content.full_text:
May 8, 1990

Sandra Taylor  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
428 J Street, Suite 600  
Sacramento, CA 95804-0814

Dear Ms. Taylor:

This will confirm our telephone conversation on May 8, 1990.

I described the following facts. My client is a candidate for office. My client has a controlled committee which is registered with the Secretary of State's office. The committee proposes to obtain an unsecured line of credit (loan) from a commercial lending institution. The line of credit (loan) would be guaranteed by the candidate and the candidate's spouse. The line of credit (loan) would be made by the commercial lending institution in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to the members of the general public.

I asked if this arrangement presents any problems under the Political Reform Act.

After checking with Jeannie Pritchard, you advised me that it would be permissible for the commercial lending institution to provide a line of credit (loan) to the committee under such circumstances. You indicated that the transaction does not need to be secured if, in the normal course of doing business, the commercial lending institution normally provides such lines of credit (loans) to people of similar financial worth. You said that there is no qualitative difference between the commercial lending institution providing a line of credit (loan) to the candidate directly, or to the candidate committee. You also indicated that there is no qualitative difference between the commercial lending institution providing a direct loan or establishing a line of credit.

You also advised that the line of credit (loan) would have to be disclosed by the candidate, should the candidate win the election for office, on a later-filed office-holder's Statement of Economic Interest.

Sandra Taylor  
May 8, 1990  
Page Two

Thank you for your prompt assistance in this matter. If is very much appreciated.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Peter A. Bagatelos

PAB/map  
feinstei:5-8taylor
